content.text:
A polynomial "splits" when it factors completely into linear factors. 
The splitting field is the smallest field extension where this occurs.

content.title: Splitting Field
content.content:
Let $f(x) \in F[x]$ be a polynomial. A field $E \supseteq F$ is a 
**splitting field** for $f$ over $F$ if:

1. $f(x)$ splits completely in $E[x]$: $f(x) = c(x - \alpha_1) 
 \cdots (x - \alpha_n)$ for $\alpha_i \in E$

2. $E = F(\alpha_1, \ldots, \alpha_n)$ (generated by the roots)

content.title: Splitting fields
content.problem:
- $f(x) = x^2 - 2$ over $\Q$: splitting field is $\Q(\sqrt{2})$

- $f(x) = x^2 + 1$ over $\R$: splitting field is $\C = \R(i)$

- $f(x) = x^3 - 2$ over $\Q$: splitting field is 
 $\Q(\sqrt[3]{2}, \omega)$ where $\omega = e^{2\pi i/3}$

- $f(x) = x^4 - 2$ over $\Q$: splitting field is 
 $\Q(\sqrt[4]{2}, i)$

content.title: Existence and Uniqueness of Splitting Fields
content.content:
For any polynomial $f(x) \in F[x]$:

1. A splitting field for $f$ over $F$ exists.

2. Any two splitting fields for $f$ over $F$ are isomorphic 
 (via an isomorphism fixing $F$).
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
Proceed by induction on $\deg(f)$. If $f$ splits in $F$, we're done. 
Otherwise, let $p(x)$ be an irreducible factor. Then $F[x]/(p(x))$ is 
a field containing a root of $p$. Repeat until $f$ splits.

content.title: Degree of splitting field of $x^3 - 2$
content.problem:
The roots of $x^3 - 2$ are $\sqrt[3]{2}, \sqrt[3]{2}\omega, 
\sqrt[3]{2}\omega^2$ where $\omega = e^{2\pi i/3}$.

The splitting field is $K = \Q(\sqrt[3]{2}, \omega)$.

By the Tower Law:
$$
 [K:\Q] &= [K:\Q(\sqrt[3]{2})] \cdot [\Q(\sqrt[3]{2}):\Q] \\
 &= [\Q(\sqrt[3]{2}, \omega):\Q(\sqrt[3]{2})] \cdot 3
$$

Since $\omega$ satisfies $x^2 + x + 1 = 0$, we have 
$[\Q(\sqrt[3]{2}, \omega):\Q(\sqrt[3]{2})] \leq 2$.

One can verify $\omega \notin \Q(\sqrt[3]{2}) \subset \R$, so the 
degree is $2$.

Thus $[K:\Q] = 2 \cdot 3 = 6$.

content.text:
The automorphisms of a splitting field that fix the base field form a 
group—the **Galois group**. This group encodes deep information 
about the polynomial's roots and is the subject of Galois theory.

content.title: Preview: Galois group of $x^2 - 2$
content.problem:
The splitting field of $x^2 - 2$ over $\Q$ is $\Q(\sqrt{2})$.

The automorphisms fixing $\Q$ are:

- Identity: $\sqrt{2} \mapsto \sqrt{2}$

- Conjugation: $\sqrt{2} \mapsto -\sqrt{2}$

The Galois group is $\Z_2$.

items: Find the splitting field of $x^4 - 1$ over $\Q$ and its degree.
Find the splitting field of $x^3 - 1$ over $\Q$.
Compute the degree of the splitting field of $x^4 - 2$ over $\Q$.

Show that the splitting field of $x^p - 1$ over $\Q$ (for 
 prime $p > 2$) has degree $p - 1$.

Find all automorphisms of $\Q(\sqrt[4]{2})/\Q$ and explain why 
 this is not the full Galois group of $x^4 - 2$.

marginNotes.text:
Unless a kernel of wheat falls to the 
ground and dies, it remains only a single seed. But if it dies, it 
produces many seeds.
